problem_summary: 2012 MCM Problem B ("Big Long River"): given a 225-mile river with Y campsites (one trip per campsite per night) and a 6-month (180-day) season, schedule the maximum number of rafting trips of varying length (7-19 rafting days / 6-18 nights, oar rafts ~4 mph vs. motor boats ~8 mph) subject to two hard wilderness constraints: no two trips share a campsite on the same night, and trips should not pass ("crossover") each other. The team must determine the river's carrying capacity and propose an optimal mix/scheduling rule for managers, while matching a target distribution of trip lengths. The team frames this as a discrete combinatorial scheduling problem and develops several original heuristics rather than a single off-the-shelf optimizer.
models_used: Naive incremental insertion algorithm (one-trip-at-a-time greedy seeding; randomly draws a trip length, tries to place it night-by-night covering equal river portions, removes a length from the distribution when it can no longer fit) — used as a baseline and explicitly discarded

Priority Model (the headline performer): schedules the whole river one NIGHT at a time rather than one trip at a time; maintains a 'last' active-trip set, with probability p sorts trips by the ratio distance_remaining/days_remaining (descending) and otherwise random-shuffles; each trip greedily targets the campsite closest to current_position + (distance_remaining/days_remaining); on contention it builds a feasible [min_camp, max_camp] travel-range window, removes taken sites, and ranks candidates primarily by whether they cause a crossover and secondarily by proximity to the ideal site

Density Perturbation Model: a continuous-relaxation method that tracks each boat's exact mile position (not campsite index), moves every boat to its equal-distance-per-day optimum, then computes a pairwise 'density vector' (a signed repulsion force, Eq. 1: rho_ij = sign(delta/d_camp) - (delta/d_camp) for boats within one campsite spacing, summed over neighbors in Eq. 2) that is iteratively subtracted to spread boats >~1 campsite apart, then rounds the continuous layout back to discrete campsites (one-to-one mapping)

Bone Growth Model (the no-crossover performer): a deterministic greedy heuristic that guarantees zero crossovers. Seeds an initial 'Bone' launch-sequence (e.g. Bones1 = [6,18,6,18,...,6,9,6], Bones2 = decreasing-length variant), exploits two heuristics — (H1) on a given launch day, order launches shortest-to-longest and require the shortest launched trip's length >= remaining days of the longest trip on the river (prevents a short trip finishing inside and crossing a long one); (H2) 'pack toward center' as the necessary feasibility test, building the schedule from the central trip outward by squeezing trajectories together — then 'fattens' the bone to saturation by greedily inserting the trip that best improves a distribution-match score, finally spreading launches in time for uniformity
Carrying-capacity metric/model C(Y) = max X(Y)/Y (max trips over season divided by number of campsites), reported as trips/campsite over 180 days
Triangle target distribution as a weighting model (weight 40 - l for trip length l) vs. a Uniform target, used to drive and evaluate distribution-matching
key_techniques: Reframing scheduling theory to show why standard tools fail: explicitly argues classical job-shop/parallel-machine scheduling and queueing theory don't fit (this is sequential jobs across UNIQUE machines = distinct river sections, with a single-slot queue and balking probability 1), justifying original heuristics
The distance_remaining / days_remaining urgency ratio as the core priority signal — an EDF/least-laxity-style rule giving precedence to trips that 'can least afford to be pushed back'
Night-by-night (column-wise) scheduling instead of trip-by-trip (row-wise) to create 'cooperation' between simultaneously-moving trips and reduce discards
Continuous relaxation + projection: solving in continuous mile-space with a physics-inspired repulsion/density field, then mapping back to the discrete campsite set
Crossover-avoidance via feasible travel-range windows ([min_camp, max_camp] from min/max daily distance) and crossover-first candidate ranking
Exploiting symmetry: the Bone model mirrors trips about a central trip, and the monotone-up/monotone-down bone sequence is an analytic feasibility construction rather than a search

A provable necessary condition for feasibility (H2 'squeeze to center'): if trips can't all be packed toward the center they can't be scheduled crossover-free, turning feasibility checking into a cheap test
Two randomized stochastic dials made explicit and tuned: sort-vs-shuffle probability p (found best in [0.3, 0.6]) and trips-per-day departures input (~12-20 optimal), with documented sensitivity
Stress-testing across a designed grid: 3 campsite densities (Y = 45, 150, 225) x 2 target distributions (Uniform, Triangle), reporting #trips, %open, avg crossovers/trip, and variance-from-target

Data-grounded assumptions: used real Colorado/Salmon River campsite spacing histograms and Grand Canyon outfitter itineraries to justify uniform campsite spacing, the 64-mile/day guaranteed motor distance, and the vessel-type/trip-length correlation that lets them ignore boat type
why_it_won: "It treated an under-determined, open-ended problem as a portfolio rather than a single answer, then was brutally honest about each model. Three genuinely original, clearly-motivated algorithms span the key trade-off axis — the Priority Model maximizes coverage (up to ~80% campsites, ~12-13 trips/campsite) but accepts crossovers, while the Bone Growth Model guarantees zero crossovers at ~50% coverage (~7.5 trips/campsite). The team didn't just pick one; they explicitly told managers to mix models over different time windows for different price/experience tiers, which directly answers the manager's real decision. Judges reward (a) recognizing why textbook scheduling/queueing theory does NOT apply and inventing appropriately, (b) quantified head-to-head comparison on a designed test grid with a clean carrying-capacity metric, (c) physical/data grounding of assumptions from real river data, and (d) candid sensitivity and limitation analysis (e.g. crossovers growing exponentially with Y, distribution skew toward short trips, bone sensitivity to seed). The continuous density-perturbation idea also signals mathematical maturity and breadth even though it underperformed. The work is decision-relevant, self-critical, and creative — exactly the Outstanding profile."
section_highlights: "Abstract/Summary: states all three models, the central coverage-vs-crossover trade-off, concrete numbers (80% vs 50% campsites; crossovers up to ~30/trip; Bone robust but seed-sensitive), and the mix-over-time recommendation — a reader knows the whole story in one paragraph. Section 4 opens by dismantling why scheduling/queueing theory doesn't apply, which earns credibility before any model appears. Models are presented as a deliberate escalation (Naive -> Priority -> Density Perturbation -> Bone Growth), each with a 'General Idea' motivation, explicit pseudocode/algorithm box, and figures showing the schedule evolving step-by-step (e.g. Fig 5's four-panel bone fattening). Section 5's single comparison table (Model x Y x Distribution with #trips, %open, avg crossovers, variance-from-distribution) lets judges compare everything at a glance. Section 6.1 is a disciplined per-model Strengths/Weaknesses list including parameter-sensitivity ranges. The closing manager Memo restates results in plain business language (70-50% coverage, 12.5-7.5 trips/campsite, mix models for pricing tiers) without jargon. Assumptions are each justified from cited real-world data rather than asserted."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Several equations and claims are loosely stated: Eq. (1)'s density function and its stated mapping range are confusingly written, and the prose has frequent typos (recieving, guarentees, neglibile) that a top-tier paper would have polished

The crossover constraint — arguably the problem's hardest requirement — is only fully honored by the Bone model; the Priority Model 'makes no attempt to resolve crossovers' beyond a local tie-break, producing up to ~29 crossovers/trip at Y=225, which somewhat undercuts the 'authentic wilderness' goal it covers 80% to achieve

The Density Perturbation Model was effectively a dead end (high crossovers, didn't beat the others) and the discrete round-back / collision discard step is hand-waved ('in the extremely rare case... one boat is discarded')
Bone Growth is a local optimum only: highly sensitive to the hand-chosen seed sequence, with no systematic search over seeds; the team admits better seeds existed but were found too late
Distribution matching is weak — all models skew toward short trips when maximizing coverage, and Bones2 'matched' only qualitatively; variance-from-target is reported but never really minimized

Strong simplifying assumptions left unrelaxed: unlimited launch/landing dock capacity, uniform speed (no rapids/weather/river-level effects), discrete nights only (intraday congestion ignored), and oar-vs-motor differentiation acknowledged but not implemented in scheduling
No global optimality bound or comparison to an integer-program/LP relaxation — carrying capacity is empirical, not proven, so 'optimal' in the title is aspirational
Promising directions (simulated annealing on the loss function; a constructed seed reaching ~100% coverage with no crossovers) are only hypothesized in the conclusion, not executed
reusable_patterns: Portfolio-of-models strategy for open-ended/under-specified problems: build a baseline plus 2-3 models that occupy opposite ends of the core trade-off, then recommend mixing them by regime — far more persuasive than betting on one algorithm
Escalation narrative: present a deliberately weak Naive model first to expose the problem's difficulties, then motivate each successive model as fixing a named failure of the previous one
Coin a precise normalized capacity metric (here C(Y) = maxX/Y, trips per campsite per season) so disparate scenarios become comparable on one axis
Use an urgency/laxity ratio (distance_remaining / days_remaining) as a greedy priority key — a transferable idea for any 'finish-by-deadline while moving through space' scheduling problem
Switch the scheduling axis (schedule per time-slot across all active agents, not per agent) to induce cooperation and cut discards — a generalizable modeling pivot

Continuous relaxation + repulsion/potential field + projection back to discrete slots: a clean recipe for discrete packing/placement problems that lets continuous optimization machinery (gradient/annealing) apply
Construct a cheap provable necessary feasibility condition (the 'pack toward center' squeeze test) and a symmetry-exploiting analytic seed so you check feasibility without full search

Justify EVERY assumption with cited real-world data (campsite-spacing histograms, outfitter itineraries) and turn one into a simplification (vessel-type ~ trip-length correlation) that legitimately collapses a dimension of the problem
Report a full sensitivity sweep with explicit usable parameter ranges (e.g. p in [0.3,0.6], 12-20 departures/day) — concrete tuning guidance reads as engineering maturity

Lay out one master comparison table (model x scenario x all metrics) plus a per-model Strengths/Weaknesses block, and close with a jargon-free manager memo that maps each model to a business decision (pricing tiers, experience quality)
